title: Disorder-Induced Phase Transitions in Altermagnetic Josephson Junctions
abstract: Altermagnetic Josephson junctions (AMJJs) can host unconventional $π$ phase and $\varphi$ phase despite vanishing net magnetizations. Whether these phases are stable against disorder existing in real materials remains an open question. Here, we investigate impact of disorder on exotic phases in two-dimensional d-wave AMJJs. We show that disorder is able to induce phase transitions between the exotic $π$ and conventional 0 phases, accompanied by a strong suppression of critical current. This behavior is attributed to modifications of the tunneling Cooper-pair phase shift and superconducting decoherence. Remarkably, the anomalous $\varphi$ phase is highly fragile in presence of disorder and can be driven to either a $π$ phase or 0 phase in a nonreciprocal manner. Across such transitions, the first harmonic of current-phase relation changes sign, while the higher-order harmonics are rapidly suppressed. Our findings reveal the crucial role of disorder in tailoring distinct phases of AMJJs and shed new light on their potential functionalities.
summary: This paper explores how disorder affects the stability of exotic superconducting phases in altermagnetic Josephson junctions. It finds that disorder can drive transitions between 0, pi, and phi phases by suppressing higher-order harmonics and reducing effective spin-splitting. These findings are crucial for understanding the potential for using altermagnets in functional superconducting devices.
main_problem: The study investigates whether the exotic pi and phi phases in altermagnetic Josephson junctions (AMJJs) are stable against the presence of disorder found in real materials.
main_result: Disorder induces phase transitions between the pi and 0 phases and causes the anomalous phi phase to be highly fragile, eventually collapsing into 0 or pi phases. This is driven by the suppression of higher-order harmonics in the current-phase relation and the reduction of effective spin-split strength.
method: The authors use a mean-field tight-binding Hamiltonian and the recursive Green's function method to calculate supercurrents, averaging results over thousands of disorder configurations.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional d-wave altermagnetic Josephson junction consisting of two s-wave superconductor leads separated by a disordered d-wave altermagnet region.
key_observables: Current-phase relation (CPR), critical supercurrent (Ic), and Fourier harmonic amplitudes (I1h, I2h, I3h).
important_parameters: Disorder strength (W), junction length (Lx), altermagnetic hopping amplitude (tJ), superconducting gap (Delta), and temperature (T).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a 2D square lattice and uses a mean-field approximation for the superconducting pairing.
figures_summary: Figures show the transition of the CPR from phi to 0 or pi phases and a Fourier analysis demonstrating the decay of higher-order harmonics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the altermagnetic Josephson junction system, describes the tight-binding model and disorder implementation, presents numerical results regarding phase transitions and supercurrent suppression, and concludes with the physical implications of disorder on phase stability.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic of the disordered AMJJ in 2D. Two s-wave superconductor leads (blue color) with a phase differ- ence ϕ are connected by a disordered d-wave AM (dx2−y2- wave or dxy-wave) with a length of Lx (red color). The color variation indicates random onsite potentials at different lat- tice sites. There are normal metals at two interfaces with a barrier potential of Vb (gray color). Periodic boundary conditions are applied along y direction. (b) Sketch of the disorder-induced phase transitions between different phases of the AMJJ. Note that the transitions between 0 phase (red color) and π phase (blue color) are reciprocal, whereas the transition from φ phase (green color) to...

FIG. 2. (a-d) Evolution of the CPR with increasing disorder strength W. In the clean limit with W = 0t, the AMJJ is in π phase for (a) with Lx = 16, 0 phase for (b) with Lx = 8, φ phase in (c) with Lx = 18, and φ phase in (d) with Lx = 14, respectively. (e-h) The critical supercurrent as a function of W corresponding to panels (a-d). Other parameters are Ly = 30, Vb = 2t, tJ = 0.3t, ∆= 0.1t, T = 0.05Tc, and µ = −2t. We have taken 5 × 103 disorder configurations for all plots.

FIG. 3. (a-b) CPRs of the AMJJ at different disorder strength W. Panel (a) demonstrates φ phase before the transition and 0 phase after the transition. The AM junction length is of Lx = 18. Panel (b) demonstrates φ phase before transition and π phase after transition. The AM junction length is of Lx = 14. (c-d) Corresponding harmonic components in CPRs as a function of W, corresponding to Fig. 2(c) and 2(d), respectively. Other parameters are the same as in Fig. 2.

FIG. 4. (a,b) The width dependence of critical supercurrent as a function of disorder strength W. The temperature is fixed at T = 0.05Tc and the junction length is Lx = 8. (c,d) The temperature dependence of critical supercurrent as a function of disorder strength W. The width of the junction is fixed at Ly = 34 and the junction length is Lx = 16. We have taken 1×104 disorder configurations for all plots. Other parameters are Vb = 2t, tJ = 0.3t, ∆= 0.1t, and µ = −2t.

FIG. 5. (a-b) Evolution of the CPR with increasing disorder strength W for dxy-wave AMs. In the clean limit, the AMJJ is in π phase for (a) with Lx = 12, and φ phase for (b) with Lx = 22. (c-d) The critical supercurrent as a function of W, corresponding to panels (a-b). Other parameters are Ly = 30, Vb = 2t, tJ = 0.3t, ∆= 0.1t, T = 0.05Tc, and µ = −2t. We have taken 5 × 103 disorder configurations for all plots.
